State Level Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY )

subdirectory_arrow_right 0 cells hidden
spark Gemini
keyboard_arrow_down

Objective(Problem statment)

  1. analyze state-wise PMFBY insurance data and identify important trends and patterns.
  2. study farmer enrollment, insured area, and seasonal crop insurance coverage.
  3. compare loanee and non-loanee farmer participation across states.
  4. analyze premium amounts collected and claims paid under the scheme.
  5. identify states with high and low claim settlement ratios.
  6. perform data cleaning, preprocessing, and transformation for accurate analysis.
  7. create visualizations and dashboards for better understanding of PMFBY performance.
  8. provide meaningful insights and recommendations for improving crop insurance implementation.
subdirectory_arrow_right 2 cells hidden
spark Gemini
  • Problem Statement

The government introduced PMFBY to protect farmers from crop losses due to natural disasters. However, the performance of the scheme varies from state to state. This project aims to understand:

  • Which states have higher farmer participation.
  • How much premium is collected and claims are paid.
  • Which states have better claim settlement performance.
  • How loanee and non-loanee farmers participate in the scheme.

spark Gemini

outcome


The project helps analyze the performance of PMFBY across different states in India.
It identifies trends in farmer enrollment, insurance coverage, premiums, and claim settlements.
The analysis provides meaningful insights through data visualization and dashboards.
The outcome supports better decision-making to improve crop insurance implementation and farmer benefits.

Domain


    

  • Agriculture
    • 

  • Insurance
    • 

  • Data Analytics
    • 



This project belongs to the Agriculture and Crop Insurance domain under State Level Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY). It focuses on analyzing agricultural insurance data to understand farmer participation, crop coverage, premium collection, and claim settlement trends.

Problem Definition and Dataset Selection


    

  • Problem Definition
    • 



The Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) scheme supports farmers through crop insurance coverage. This project analyzes state-wise insurance data to understand claims, premiums, and farmer participation trends.


    

  • Expected Outcome
    • 



The project helps identify meaningful patterns and trends in crop insurance data. It also supports better agricultural planning through insights and visualizations.


    

  • Dataset Source, Size, and Features
    • 



The dataset includes multiple rows and columns related to crop insurance performance. Features include state name, premium amount, claims paid, and farmer enrollment details.


    

  • Import Libraries and Load Dataset
    • 



Python libraries such as Pandas, NumPy, Matplotlib, and Seaborn are used for analysis. The dataset is loaded using the read_csv() function.


    

  • Initial EDA
    • 



Initial Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) helps understand the dataset structure and quality. Functions like head(), describe(), null checks, and duplicate checks are used for analysis.

        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          18 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
 Dataset Description






Column Name
Description





id
Unique record ID




year
Insurance year




season
Crop season (Kharif/Rabi)




scheme
Insurance scheme name




state_name
State name




state_code
State code




district_count
Number of districts covered




farmer_count
Total farmers enrolled




loanee
Farmers with loans




non_loanee
Farmers without loans




transgender
Transgender farmer percentage




sc
Scheduled Caste percentage




st
Scheduled Tribe percentage




obc
OBC farmer percentage




gen
General category percentage




marginal
Marginal farmers percentage




small
Small farmers percentage




other
Other farmer categories




iu_count
Insurance unit count




gross_premium
Total premium amount

output


    

  • The summary statistics show the distribution of numerical data in the Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana dataset.
    

    • 

  • Count (286) → There are 286 records in each column, meaning no major missing data.
    

    • 

  • Mean → Shows the average value , average farmer count, premium, and insured area across states.
    

    • 

  • Standard Deviation (std) → Measures data spread; high values show large variation between states.
    

    • 

  • Min → The smallest value in each column (some states have very low or zero values).
    

    • 

  • 25% (Q1) → 25% of data falls below this value (lower range of states).
50% (Median) → Middle value of dataset, gives a better central tendency than mean.
    

    • 

  • 75% (Q3) → 75% of values fall below this point (upper range of states).
Max → Highest values show extreme cases like highly populated or high-claim states.
    

    • 

  • The dataset shows high variation across states, especially in farmer count, insured area, and gross premium, indicate.

keyboard_arrow_down
Data Cleaning and Pre-processing


    

  • Handle missing values
    

     Missing values are empty or null data present in the dataset. These values are checked using isnull() and handled by replacing them with suitable values or removing them to improve data quality.
    

    • 

  • Handle Duplicates
    

     Duplicate records are repeated rows in the dataset. These duplicates can affect analysis accuracy, so they are identified using duplicated() and removed using drop_duplicates().
    

    • 

  • Rename Columns or Drop Unwanted Columns
    

    Some column names may be unclear or unnecessary for analysis. Renaming columns improves readability, while unwanted columns like id can be removed to simplify the dataset.
    

    • 

  • Treat outliers
    

    Outliers are extreme or unusual values that differ greatly from other data points. They are identified using boxplots and treated if necessary to avoid incorrect analysis results.
    

    • 

  • Check skewness and apply transformations
    

    Skewness shows whether data distribution is balanced or uneven. Highly skewed data can affect analysis, so transformations like log transformation are applied to normalize the data.
    

    • 

  • Convert data types
    

    Data type conversion ensures columns are stored in the correct format such as integer, float, or datetime. This helps perform accurate calculations and analysis.
    

    • 

  • Feature transformations
    

    Feature transformation creates new useful columns from existing data. For example, extracting year and month from the date column helps analyze seasonal and yearly trends in PMFBY data.
    

    •

                   "farmor_count":"total_former",
                   "lonee":"loan_formers",
                   "non_loanee":"non_loan_former",
                   "gross_premium":"premium_amount",
                   "district_count":"total_districts",
                   "iu_count":"insurance_units",
                   "sum_insured":"insured_amount",
                   "farmer_share":"farmer_contribution",
                   "goi_share":"central_share",
                  "state_share":"state_government_share"},
                  inplace=True)

 Outlier Detection and Removal

        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          0 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    

      

        

          
          

            
          

        

      
#  Outlier Detection and Removal
#  Detect Outliers in premium_amount
Q1 = df['premium_amount'].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = df['premium_amount'].quantile(0.75)
IQR = Q3 - Q1
LB = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
UB = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

Q1,Q3,IQR,LB,UB

    
 
 

        

          

          

            
 
(np.float64(269.34999999999997),
 np.float64(43329.025),
 np.float64(43059.675),
 np.float64(-64320.162500000006),
 np.float64(107918.5375))

          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
Output


    

  • Outliers in premium_amount are identified successfully.
    • 

  • A few states may have unusually high premium amounts compared to others.
    • 

  • These extreme values can be reviewed, treated, or removed if necessary.
    • 

  • Detecting outliers helps create a more reliable PMFBY analysis and dashboard.
    • 


        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          0 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
remove outliers

        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          0 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    

      

        

          
          

            
          

        

      
Outliers = df[(df['premium_amount'] <LB) | (df['premium_amount'] > UB)]
Outliers

    
 
 

        

          

          

            
 

          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    

      

        

          
          

            
          

        

      
outlier = df[(df['premium_amount'] > LB) | (df['premium_amount'] <UB)]

    
 
 

        

          
 

          

            
 

          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
Remove outlier

        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          0 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    

      

        

          
          

            
          

        

      

df = df[(df['premium_amount'] >= LB)&
        (df['premium_amount'] <= UB)]

print("Shape after removing outliers:", df.shape)

    
 
 

        

          

          

            
 
Shape after removing outliers: (246, 27)


          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
output


Outlier Removal  Explanation


    

  • Outlier removal is the process of identifying and removing extreme values    that are significantly different from the rest of the data.
    

    • 

  • Why Remove Outliers?
    

     can distort statistical results such as mean and standard deviation.
 They can affect visualizations and hide actual data patterns.
 outliers improves the accuracy of analysis and decision-making.
 PMFBY Dataset
    

    • 

  • In the PMFBY dataset, columns such as premium_amount, loanee, non_loan_former, and insurance_units may contain extreme values due to differences among states.
    

    • 


        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          0 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    

      

        

          
          

            
          

        

      
df[num_col].kurtosis()

    
 
 

        

          

          

            
 

          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    

      

        

          
          

            
          

        

      
# Boxplot after outlier removal
plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
sns.boxplot(df["premium_amount"])

plt.title("Box Plot After Outlier Removal ")
plt.xlabel("premium_amount")
plt.show()

    
 
 

        

          

          

            
 

          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
Output


    

  • The first box plot shows extreme premium values as outliers.
    

    • 

  • The IQR method removes values outside the acceptable range.
    

    • 

  • The second box plot shows a cleaner distribution with reduced extreme values.
    

    • 

  • The cleaned dataset is more suitable for EDA, visualization, and Power BI dashboard creation.
    

    • 

  • Premium Amount There are many outliers above the upper whisker. This means a few states/districts have very high premium amounts compared to the majority. The data is right-skewed.
    

    • 


        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          0 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    

      

        

          
          

            
          

        

      
outlier_col = ['loanee',
               'non_loan_former',
               'insurance_units',
               'premium_amount']

for col in outlier_col:
    Q1 = df[col].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = df[col].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1

    LB = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    UB = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

    df = df[(df[col] >= LB) & (df[col] <= UB)]

    print(f"Shape after removing outliers from {col}:", df.shape)

    
 
 

        

          

          

            
 
Shape after removing outliers from loanee: (207, 27)
Shape after removing outliers from non_loan_former: (175, 27)
Shape after removing outliers from insurance_units: (156, 27)
Shape after removing outliers from premium_amount: (137, 27)


          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini

keyboard_arrow_down
EDA and Visualizations


    

  • Univariate Analysis:
    • 



Univariate analysis is used to study the distribution and characteristics of individual PMFBY variables such as farmer count, premium amount, and crop season using histograms, countplots, and boxplots.


    

  • Bivariate Analysis:
    • 



Bivariate analysis helps examine the relationship between two PMFBY variables, such as farmer enrollment and premium amount, using scatterplots, barplots, and correlation heatmaps.


    

  • Multivariate Analysis:
    • 



Multivariate analysis is used to understand the interaction among multiple PMFBY variables, including farmer count, insurance units, premium amount, and season through pairplots, grouped analysis, and pivot tables.


    

  • Visualization Interpretation:
    • 



Each visualization is interpreted to identify meaningful trends, patterns, and relationships that support decision-making in crop insurance management.


    

  • Business Story Focus:
    • 



The analysis focuses on understanding farmer participation, insurance coverage, premium collection, and seasonal trends to provide actionable insights for improving PMFBY implementation across states.


Separate Visualization Cells:


Each chart is created in a separate code cell to ensure clear presentation, easy interpretation, and better project documumentation

        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          0 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini

# 1. Histogram – Farmer Count Distribution
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.histplot(df["farmer_count"], bins=30, kde=True)


plt.title("distribution former count")
plt.xlabel("farmer count")
plt.ylabel("frequency")
plt.show()

    
 
 

        

          

          

            
 

          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
*output


    

  • The histogram shows that most farmer count values are concentrated at the lower range (0–10,000).
    • 

  • The distribution has a long tail on the right side, meaning a few states have very high farmer counts.
    • 

  • This indicates the data is positively skewed (right-skewed) and is not normally distributed.
    • 

  • The high values may represent states with exceptionally large farmer populations, which act as potential outliers.
    • 

  • output
    • 



The farmer count distribution is right-skewed, with most observations having low values and a few states showing extremely high farmer counts, resulting in a long right tail and potential outliers."

        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          0 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    

      

        

          
          

            
          

        

      
# Countplot – Crop Season Distribution
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.countplot(x=df["season"])
plt.title("season distribution")
plt.xlabel("season")
plt.ylabel("count")
plt.show()

    
 
 

        

          

          

            
 

          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
output


    

  • The bar chart shows the distribution of data across two seasons: Kharif and Rabi.
    • 

  • Kharif season has a slightly higher count (around 71) compared to Rabi season (around 66).
    • 

  • The difference between the two seasons is small, indicating that the dataset is almost evenly distributed across both seasons.
    • 

  • This suggests that crop insurance participation is balanced between Kharif and Rabi seasons, with a slightly higher focus on Kharif.
    • 



The season distribution is nearly balanced, with Kharif having a slightly higher number of records than Rabi, indicating similar crop insurance coverage in both seasons.

    •

    Most premium amounts are concentrated within a certain range, while a few records have very high premium values (outliers).
    

    • 

  • What features you used?
    

    premium_amount
    

    • 

  • From this what are you showing us?
    

    The spread, variability, and extreme values of premium amounts collected under the PMFBY scheme.

#  Scatterplot – Farmer Count vs Premium Amount
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.scatterplot(x='farmer_count', y='premium_amount', data=df)
plt.title("Farmer Count vs Premium Amount")
plt.xlabel("Farmer Count")
plt.ylabel("Premium Amount")
plt.show()


    
 
 

        

          

          

            
 

          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
output


    

  • The scatter plot shows the relationship between Farmer Count and Premium Amount.
The points are widely scattered, indicating there is no strong * linear relationship between the two variables.
Generally, as farmer count increases, the premium amount tends to increase, but there are many exceptions.
    • 

  • Some points are far from the main cluster, indicating outliers with unusually high premium amounts or farmer counts.
    • 



output


"The scatter plot suggests a weak positive relationship between farmer count and premium amount, with significant variability and the presence of outliers."

# Barplot Top 10 States by Farmer Count
top10 = df.groupby('state')['farmer_count'].sum().sort_values(ascending=False).head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
top10.plot(kind='bar')
plt.title("Top 10 States by Farmer Enrollment")
plt.xlabel("State")
plt.ylabel("Farmer Count")
plt.show()

    
 
 

        

          

          

            
 

          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
keyboard_arrow_down
Interpretation of the Above Chart


    

  • 

  • This bar chart shows the Top 10 States by Farmer Enrollment under the PMFBY scheme.
    • 

  • Himachal Pradesh has the highest farmer enrollment with around 9.2 lakh farmers, followed by Uttarakhand with about 5.7 lakh farmers.
States like Assam, Chhattisgarh, and Puducherry have comparatively lower enrollments.
    • 

  • The large variation indicates that farmer participation in the insurance scheme differs significantly across states.
    • 



output


 Himachal Pradesh and Uttarakhand have the highest farmer enrollment, while states like Puducherry and Chhattisgarh have lower participation, showing uneven adoption of the PMFBY scheme across states.

        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          1 cell hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    

      

        

          
          

            
          

        

      
# Correlation Heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(12,8))
sns.heatmap(df.corr(numeric_only=True), annot=False, cmap='coolwarm')
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap")
plt.show()

    
 
 

        

          

          

            
 

          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
  Correlation Heatmap


    

  • The heatmap shows the correlation between all numerical variables in the PMFBY dataset.
    • 

  • Dark red (close to +1) indicates a strong positive relationship, while dark blue (close to -1) indicates a strong negative relationship.
    • 

  • insured_amount, farmer_contribution, central_share, and premium_amount have a strong positive correlation, meaning these variables increase together.
    • 

  • Most other variables have weak or moderate correlations, indicating they do not strongly influence each other.
    • 



keyboard_arrow_down
Key Insights:


 Premium Amount ↔ Central Share → Strong positive correlation.
 Farmer Count ↔ Insured Amount → Moderate positive correlation.
 Male ↔ Female → Strong negative correlation in some records.
 Marginal, Small, and Other farmers show weak to moderate relationships.


    

  • The correlation heatmap helps identify relationships between variables, and it shows that premium amount, insured amount, and government contributions are strongly positively correlated, while most other variables have weak correlations.
    • 


        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          3 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    

      

        

          
          

            
          

        

      

# bie chart
season_count = df['season'].value_counts()

plt.figure(figsize=(6,6))
plt.pie(season_count,
        labels=season_count.index,
        autopct='%1.1f%%')

plt.title("season_distribution")
plt.show()

    
 
 

        

          

          

            
 

          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
output


    

  • The pie chart shows the distribution of records between the Kharif and Rabi seasons.
    • 

  • Kharif accounts for 51.8% of the total records, while Rabi accounts for 48.2%.
    • 

  • The percentages are very close, indicating that the dataset is almost evenly distributed across both seasons.
    • 

  • This suggests that the PMFBY scheme provides similar coverage in both crop seasons, with a slightly higher participation during Kharif.
    • 



*The season distribution is nearly balanced, with Kharif contributing 51.8% and Rabi 48.2% of the records, indicating almost equal crop insurance participation in both seasons."

output


    

  • The pair plot shows the relationship between farmer_count, premium_amount, and insurance_units.
    • 

  • The diagonal histograms indicate that all three variables are right-skewed, with most values concentrated at lower ranges.
    • 

  • The scatter plots show weak to moderate positive relationships, meaning that as farmer count increases, premium amount and insurance units tend to increase, but not consistently.
    • 

  • A few points are far from the main cluster, indicating the presence of outliers.
Key Insight:
    • 



Most farmers have low premium amounts and insurance units, while a few records have very high values, causing skewness and outliers in the data.


The pair plot helps identify distributions and relationships among variables and shows right-skewed data with weak positive correlations and some significant outliers.

plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.lineplot(x=df.index, y=df["farmer_count"])
plt.title("Farmer Count Trend")
plt.xlabel("Index")
plt.ylabel("Farmer Count")
plt.show()

    
 
 

        

          

          

            
 

          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
output


    

  • The line has many sharp peaks and drops, which means the farmer count varies  significantly across observations.
    • 

  • Most values are below 20,000 farmers, but some observations reach 80,000 –  95,000 farmers.
    • 

  • There is no clear increasing or decreasing trend because the data fluctuates heavily.
    • 



expaination


 The line chart shows that farmer enrollment is highly uneven across the dataset, with several observations having exceptionally high farmer counts.
 The frequent spikes indicate significant variation in farmer participation between different records.

                      'premium_amount',
                      'insurance_units'
                      ]].mean()

    
 
 

        

          

          

            
 

          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
    

  • output
    

    • 

  • Compares average farmer participation and premium collection by season.
    

    • 

  • Helps understand seasonal demand for crop insurance.
    

    • 

  • Supports planning for Kharif and Rabi seasons.
    

    •

keyboard_arrow_down
Interpretation of the Above Analysis


    

  • Explain the Table:
    • 



This grouped analysis shows the average farmer count, premium amount, and insurance units for each crop season.


    

  • What is it saying?
    • 



It compares the performance of different seasons based on farmer participation and insurance activity.


    

  • What features you used?
    • 



season, farmer_count, premium_amount, insurance_units


    

  • From this what are you showing us?
    • 



Which season has higher average farmer enrollment, premium collection, and insurance coverage under the PMFBY scheme.

 Pivot Table  State vs Season Premium Analysis

        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          0 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    

      

        

          
          

            
          

        

      
pivot_table = pd.pivot_table(
    df,
    values='premium_amount',
    index='state',
    columns='season',
    aggfunc='sum'
)

pivot_table.head()

    
 
 

        

          
 

          

            
 

          

        

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
keyboard_arrow_down
Interpretation of the Above Analysis


    

  • Explain the Table:
    • 



This pivot table shows the total premium amount collected by each state across different crop seasons.


    

  • What is it saying?
    • 



It helps compare premium collection between seasons for each state.


    

  • What features you used?
    • 



state, season, premium_amount


    

  • From this what are you showing us?
    • 



The seasonal premium contribution of each state and which states generate higher insurance premiums.

        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          6 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
keyboard_arrow_down
Note for Kind of Analysis to be Done


Descriptive Analysis


    

  • Summarizes the PMFBY dataset using statistical measures and visualizations.
    • 

  • Helps understand farmer enrollment, premium collection, insurance coverage,   and seasonal distribution across states.
    • 



Diagnostic Analysis


    

  • Identifies reasons behind patterns and trends observed in the dataset.
    • 

  • Helps determine why certain states have higher farmer participation premium   amounts, or insurance coverage than others.
    • 



Predictive Analysis


    

  • Uses historical PMFBY data to forecast future trends in farmer enrollment, premium collection, or insurance coverage.
    • 

  • Supports planning and decision-making through basic forecasting techniques.
    • 



Prescriptive Analysis


    

  • Provides recommendations based on the insights obtained from the analysis.
    • 

  • Helps policymakers improve farmer participation, insurance coverage, and   overall effectiveness of the PMFBY scheme.
    •

          0 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini

  • Cleaned and transformed the dataset in Google Colab using Python.
    • 

  • Exported the dataset as PMFBY_State_Level_Analysis.csv.
    • 

  • Imported the CSV file into Power BI.
    • 

  • Created relationships and calculated measures (KPIs).
    • 

  • Designed an interactive dashboard containing:
    • 

  • Farmer Enrollment Trend
    • 

  • Season-wise Distribution
    • 

  • State-wise Premium Analysis
    • 

  • Correlation Analysis
*Insurance Coverage Analysis
    • 

  • Top States by Farmer Participation

Documentation, Insights and Presentation Dashboard Summary


The Power BI dashboard shows the performance of PMFBY across different states and seasons in India. It helps understand farmer participation, premium collection, and insurance coverage.

        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          0 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
keyboard_arrow_down
Key Insights


    

  • A few states have very high farmer participation, while some states have low participation.
    • 

  • States with more farmers generally collect higher premium amounts.
    • 

  • Kharif season has the highest insurance participation.
    • 

  • Premium amounts and insurance coverage vary significantly across states.
    • 

  • Some states have unusually high values, which appear as outliers.
    •

          3 cells hidden

keyboard_arrow_down
Recommendations


    

  • Increase Awareness Programs
    • 



Conduct awareness campaigns in low-performing states to improve farmer participation.


    

  • Encourage Non-Loanee Farmers
    • 



Introduce special initiatives to increase insurance enrollment among non-loanee farmers.


    

  • Improve Data Monitoring
    • 



Use automated dashboards and regular reporting for better monitoring of PMFBY performance.


    

  • Strengthen Claim Settlement Process
    • 



Improve claim processing efficiency to increase trust among farmers.


    

  • Use Predictive Analytics
    • 



Apply forecasting models to predict future enrollment and premium trends.

        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          0 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
keyboard_arrow_down
Final Story of the Dashboard


The dashboard shows that PMFBY is helping many farmers through crop insurance, but participation is not equal across all states. Some states perform very well, while others need improvement. The analysis helps identify areas where the government can increase awareness and improve insurance covera

        

      


      

        
        

          subdirectory_arrow_right
          0 cells hidden
        

      

      

    

      spark
      Gemini
    
 

      

        

        

          
keyboard_arrow_down
Conclusion


This project used Python and Power BI to analyze PMFBY data. The analysis identified important trends in farmer enrollment, premium collection, and insurance coverage. The dashboard provides useful insights for better decision-making and improving the PMFBY scheme.
